citation.title: Dopamine-initiated graft copolymerization of a series of methacrylate monomers on ultrafiltration membranes for efficient dye–salt separation

content: A B S T R A C T Bio-inspired dopamine is widely used to modify membrane surfaces due to its excellent adhesion and selfpolymerization properties. However, dopamine deposition using oxidizing agents typically produces a nonuniform, dense layer with low permeability, which reduces the permeate flux of the membrane. Here, we developed high-flux antifouling membranes via a simple one-step graft copolymerization of dopamine with a series of hydrophilic methacrylate monomers to modify a commercial polyacrylonitrile ultrafiltration membrane. Grafting with methacrylate monomers reduced polydopamine aggregation and enabled the formation of a ho­mogeneous reaction solution. Unlike conventional methacrylate grafting methods that require redox or photo­initiators, our approach utilizes polydopamine as the initiator to achieve robust graft copolymerization. The resulting membrane modified with dopamine and pseudo-zwitterionic 3-sulfopropyl methacrylate-co-[2- (methacryloyloxy)ethyl]trimethyl ammonium (pDA-SPM-MOETMA) exhibited excellent Congo Red rejection (99.9 %) from Congo Red/NaCl binary feed solution and high water permeance (238 ± 12 L m-2 h-1 bar-1). The pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane also showed excellent antifouling properties for bovine serum albumin/NaCl solution and long-term performance filtration stability for Congo Red/NaCl feed solution with high flux recovery ratios of 97.3 % and 99.7 %, respectively.

This membrane modification strategy, based on polydopamineinitiated polymethacrylate grafting, offers a promising route for efficient dye–salt separation in treating waste­water from the dye and textile industries.

content: 1. Introduction With the rapid growth of the global population, the scarcity of potable water has become a critical challenge that must be addressed urgently [1]. Effluents from various industries, such as the textile, leather, and pharmaceutical industries, contain organic and inorganic contaminants, which pollute water resources [2]. The textile industry is a major contributor to pollution by discharging wastewater with high concentrations of toxic synthetic dyes and inorganic salts. Most syn­thetic dyes, such as Sudan Red and Congo Red, are resistant to degra­dation and are toxic and carcinogenic, causing serious damage to aquatic life, water bodies, and human health [3]. Adsorption, flocculation, chemical oxidation, and biological treat­ment methods for wastewater from the dye industry require high energy input, high capital cost, complicated operating conditions, and the use of toxic chemicals [4]. In contrast, membrane-based separation methods are promising due to their low energy demand, easy operation, and low chemical consumption. Nanofiltration membranes have transport properties suitable for dye–salt separation; their pore size of <2 nm separates many of the commonly used organic dyes and rejects divalent ions [5]. Ultrafiltration membranes with larger pore sizes are less effi­cient for dye separation. Therefore, ideally, a membrane would reject dyes and have high divalent ion transport and high permeate flux.

However, membrane fouling by the deposition of organic matter and biomolecules on the surface is a major obstacle in the efficient treatment of waste effluents from the dye industry. Therefore, antifouling and hydrophilic properties of the membrane are crucial for improving membrane performance, which may be achieved by surface functionalization. Mussel-inspired polydopamine (pDA) has been widely used as a coating material for forming a thin selective layer over porous polymeric supports [6,7]. Dopamine is self-polymerized by oxidation in an alkaline medium, and its catechol and amine functional groups are used for immobilizing synthetic monomers and nanomaterials and for chelating

The addition of nanomaterials in a polymer matrix im­proves the membrane flux at the cost of membrane rejection efficiency [8–10]. The addition of different oxidizing reagents, such as CuSO4/H2O2 [11,12], NaIO4 [13], ammonium persulfate [14], and Fe3+/H2O2 [15], enables the rapid codeposition of pDA with other functional materials and reduces the reaction time. However, oxidizing agents compromise membrane performance by creating pDA aggregates and a thick nonuniform layer that decreases the membrane permeate flux substantially. Moreover, the hydrophobic aromatic groups of pDA counteract the hydrophilic catechol, quinone amine, and imine groups, which limits the antifouling behavior of pDA [16]. Our previous studies have demonstrated that the grafting of zwit­terionic [17], oppositely charged (pseudo-zwitterionic) [18,19], and polyethyleneglycol (PEG) derivatives [20] of methacrylate monomers over polymeric membranes forms a hydrophilic layer, leading to the formation of antifouling and antibacterial surfaces. Zhang et al. used the semiquinone radical of dopamine as an initiator to polymerize acrylate monomers on a silicon wafer and examined bacterial adhesion on the modified surface [21].

content: e radical of dopamine as an initiator to polymerize acrylate monomers on a silicon wafer and examined bacterial adhesion on the modified surface [21]. The main advantages of the methacrylate monomers are their flexible backbone and hydrophilic and antifouling functionalities, and these monomers can be grafted to pDA by aza-Michael addition and cation–π interpolymer interactions. Zwitter­ionic sulfobetaine methacrylate (SBMA) [22], polyethyleneimine (PEI) [23], and CuNP@PEI [24] have been codeposited with dopamine on a hydrolyzed polyacrylonitrile (PAN) membrane using Cu2SO4/H2O2. This method has the advantage of requiring a single step for coating. However, codeposition produces membranes with low permeate flux, probably resulting from the thick pDA layer. Therefore, further devel­opment of single-step codeposition is required to increase the permeate flux and overcome the permeability/selectivity trade-off. In this study, dopamine-initiated graft copolymerization of a series of hydrophilic methacrylate monomers was performed onto a commercial ultrafiltration PAN membrane. Because membrane surface properties are crucial for performance, methacrylate monomers that were posi­tively charged, zwitterionic, pseudo-zwitterionic, neutral, or negatively charged were grafted to identify the optimal hydrogel chemistry. The goal was to develop highly efficient membranes for dye–salt separation.

content: egatively charged were grafted to identify the optimal hydrogel chemistry. The goal was to develop highly efficient membranes for dye–salt separation. The dopamine catechol and amino groups provided adhesion sites on the membrane surface for grafting, and the formation of polymethacrylate contributed strong electrostatic forces that supported the formation of an antifouling hydration layer. The modified membranes were tested for the removal of organic dyes with high permeate flux and high divalent ion transport. While a previous study demonstrated one-step dopamineinitiated methacrylate copolymerization on silicon wafers [21], to the best of our knowledge, this is the first report applying this approach to porous ultrafiltration membranes for water treatment. By systematically evaluating a range of methacrylate monomers with different chemis­tries, we established structure–performance relationships that led to enhanced antifouling, high water flux, and efficient dye–salt separation. The graft polymerization was confirmed by attenuated total reflectan­ce–Fourier transform infrared (ATR–FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and the physicochemical properties of the prepared membranes were thoroughly investigated. The modified membranes demonstrated excellent dye rejection, including from Congo Red/NaCl binary solutions, along with high permeate flux and superior antifouling performance.

[2-(Methoxymethyl)ethyl)]trimethyl ammonium chloride solution (MOETMA; 75 wt % in H2O) was obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA), SBMA (98 %) was obtained from Angene Chemical, and 3sulfopropyl methacrylate potassium salt (SPM; >97 %) was purchased from TCI. Magnesium sulfate was purchased from Alfa Aesar, sodium chloride was purchased from Bio-Lab (Israel), and magnesium chloride and sodium sulfate were obtained from Carlo Erba (Italy). Milli-Q water was used for all the synthetic and filtration experiments. 2.2. pDA coating on the ultrafiltration PAN membrane Dopamine hydrochloride was polymerized on the PAN ultrafiltration substrate by air deposition [20]. Nascent PAN membranes were washed with isopropanol/water before functionalizing the membrane surface. 10 mM Tris buffer (12.1 mg) was dissolved in deionized (DI) water (100 mL), and the pH of the solution was set to 8.5. Dopamine hydrochloride (0.2 g) was added to the buffer solution, and the solution was immedi­ately poured onto the PAN substrate fixed in a holder so that dopamine polymerization only occurred on the active surface of the membrane. To allow the oxidative polymerization to reach completion, the reaction was performed in air for 10 h with orbital shaking at 200 rpm (Heidolph Unimax 1010, Germany).

Finally, the pDA-coated membranes were washed thoroughly with DI water and ethanol to remove the dopamine aggregates and unreacted reagents. The membranes were stored in water at 4 ◦C until further use and characterization. 2.3. pDA-methacrylate graft copolymerization on the PAN membrane For the dopamine–methacrylate graft copolymerization, dopamine and methacrylate monomers were used in a 1:1 wt ratio (Table 1). Dopamine (0.2 g) was dissolved in Tris buffer solution (50 mL), and methacrylate monomers (0.2 g) were dissolved separately in Tris buffer solution (50 mL) at pH 8.5. The two solutions were mixed and imme­diately poured over the PAN substrate and allowed to polymerize at RT for 10 h with orbital shaking at 200 rpm. The membranes were washed thoroughly with DI water and named pDA-HEMA, pDA-SBMA, pDAMOETMA, pDA-SPM, and pDA-SPM-MOETMA (Table 1). 2.4. content: 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Membrane surface modification and characterization Dopamine-initiated methacrylate graft polymerization was per­formed as shown in Fig. 1. The surface morphology of the membranes was investigated by SEM analysis. The SEM images of pristine PAN, pDA, and pDA-SPM-MOETMA membranes are shown in Fig. 2. Pores were observed on the surface of all membranes; however, there were fewer surface pores on the pDA membrane (Fig. 2B) than on the pristine PAN membrane (Fig. 2A and D), implying that the pDA deposition on the porous PAN support narrowed and partially covered the membrane pores. The magnified SEM image (Fig. 2E) shows that aggregated pDA nanoparticles started appearing on the membrane surface, which in­dicates non-uniform pDA deposition. The pDA-initiated graft copolymerization of SPM-MOETMA resulted in a uniform coating (Fig. 2C and F), and the size of the membrane surface pores was reduced substantially (Fig. 2F). Due to covalent in­teractions and non-covalent interactions, such as hydrogen bonding and π–π stacking, pDA molecules tended to aggregate and deposit nonuniformly on the membrane surface. However, the presence of poly (SPM-MOETMA) weakened the π–π stacking and charge transfer in­teractions between pDA molecules and increased the uniformity of the pDA-methacrylate deposition [21]. Hence, the pDA-methacrylate copolymerization produced a uniformly coated membrane surface.

content: sed the uniformity of the pDA-methacrylate deposition [21]. Hence, the pDA-methacrylate copolymerization produced a uniformly coated membrane surface. Photographs of the pDA and pDA-SPM-MOETMA reaction solutions are shown in Fig. 3. For pDA, the initial transparent reaction solution at 0.5 h turned dark brown after 5 h due to the polymerization of the dopamine molecules, and after 10 h the aggregated pDA nanoparticles started to precipitate due to their aggregation. This aggregation was probably caused by the covalent and non-covalent interactions of the free hydroxyl groups of catechol and π–π stacking of dopamine mole­cules. However, the pDA-SPM-MOETMA reaction solution remained homogeneous, and no aggregation and precipitation were observed, even after 10 h. The presence of methacrylate monomers with dopamine weakened the π–π stacking and charge transfer interactions among pDA molecules, leading to a non-aggregated, homogeneous reaction solution. The surface hydrophilicity of polymeric membranes strongly affects the membrane filtration and antifouling performance. The free energy of hydration, surface roughness, and water contact angle values of the pristine PAN and modified membranes are provided in Table 2. The pristine PAN membrane had the highest contact angle value of 60 ± 5◦, which decreased in the order 55◦> 52◦> 50◦> 48◦> 46◦> 42◦for the modified membranes pDA, pDA-MOETMA pDA-SBMA, pDA-HEMA, pDA-SPM, and pDA-SPM-MOETMA, respectively (Fig. 4A).

The lowest value of water contact angle was observed for the pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane, which was attributed to the hydrophilicity of the pseudozwitterionic SPM and MOETMA monomer grafting [28]. Membrane surfaces with higher roughness tend to show lower values of water contact angle [29]. As stated by Van Oss [30], materials with ΔGSW of less than or equal to -113 mJ m-2 are true hydrophilic materials. pDA-SPM-MOETMA was more hydrophilic than the other modified membranes because the ΔGSW value of -124.5 mJ m-2 was closer to the characteristic value of its free energy of hydration. The hydration layer formed by hydrophilic poly-SPM-MOETMA grafting led to higher water permeability and higher resistance to foulants by decreasing the inter­action of the foulants with the membrane surface. Atomic force micro­scopy images of pristine PAN, pDA, and pDA-SPM-MOETMA membranes are provided in Fig. S1. The pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane showed a slightly higher average roughness (Ra) value of 8.35 nm than the pDA membrane (6.3 nm; Table 2), indicating the presence of methacrylate fragments with higher coating density. The surface charges of the pristine PAN and pDA-methacrylate membranes were analyzed by measuring the streaming current poten­tial at different pH values (Fig. S2). The MWCO values (90 % rejection of the solute) were 130, 42, and 17 kDa for the pristine PAN, pDA, and pDA-SPM-MOETMA membranes, respectively (Table 4). The mean pore radius (rp) of the membranes was calculated based on the measured MWCO values of the membranes (Table 4). pDA and pDA-SPM-MOETMA membranes had mean pore radii of ~4.8 and ~3.3 nm, which were ~1.6 and ~2.4 times smaller than that of the pristine PAN membrane (8 nm), respectively. The pDA and pDA-SPM-MOETMA deposition, which probably occurred inside the membrane pores, probably narrowed the pores in addition to coating the surface. The pore narrowing in the modified membranes was consistent with the SEM results. The deposit density of the membranes was measured as 0.76 mg cm-2 for the pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane and 0.41 mg cm-2 for the pDA membrane, indicating a 1.8-times higher surface deposition for the pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane and intrusion within the microporous structure. The water content of the membranes was also measured (Table 4). The pDA and pDA-SPM-MOETMA mem­branes exhibited higher water contents of 85.3 % and 93.5 %, respec­tively, compared with the pristine PAN membrane (60 %). The higher water content of the pDA membrane was due to the hydrophilicity of pDA.

Divalent ions typically retain stronger hydration shells due to their higher charge density and larger hydrated sizes [48], which makes them more effectively excluded by the tight pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane. Donnan exclusion also contributes, as the pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane surface, though modified with both anionic (SO3-) and cationic (N+) groups, remains overall negatively charged (Fig. S2). It disfavors the passage of co-ions carrying the same charge as the membrane surface, especially multiva­lent ions like SO42-. Dielectric exclusion may further limit ion passage, particularly for divalent ions, due to the lower dielectric constant of the membrane matrix compared with water. The combined effect of these mechanisms explains the higher rejection of MgSO4 and Na2SO4.

Long-term stability and membrane performance under various operating pressures The long-term stability of the pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane was evaluated by 60 h filtration of Congo Red/NaCl binary solution (Fig. 10A). The filtration experiment was performed in a dead-end cell, and the permeate was added back to the feed solution to maintain the feed concentration. The rejection for the Congo Red dye was consistently high (>98 %) throughout the filtration experiment, indicating robust, stable membrane performance. During the initial 10 h, the permeate flux was decreased slightly by 7 %, probably due to initial dye adsorption causing fouling. However, from 10 to 60 h, the permeation remained high (196 ± 2 L m-2 h-1), and no further decrease was observed, con­firming the stable performance of the pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane. FTIR analysis of the pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane before and after 60 h filtration with a Congo Red/NaCl solution showed no significant change in the characteristic IR peaks, indicating preservation of the membrane’s chemical structure (Fig. S5). A slight decrease in peak in­tensities was observed, which may be attributed to surface adsorption of dye molecules during filtration, partially masking the IR absorption signals. In addition to the long-term stability shown here, the reusability and cleaning performance of the pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane were evaluated separately through repeated fouling tests using BSA and Congo Red solutions (see Section 3.7).

Antifouling performance of the pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane The antifouling performance of the membranes was examined by filtration experiments with BSA protein (100 ppm)/NaCl (5 mM) and Congo Red dye (100 ppm)/NaCl (15 mM) binary solutions at 1 bar. BSA protein is a biofoulant that limits the membrane separation performance through biofouling caused by the accumulation and adsorption of pro­tein molecules at the membrane surface. Fig. 11A shows the antifouling behavior of the membranes during three successive filtration cycles of BSA (100 ppm)/NaCl (5 mM), where the membrane surface was washed with water after each cycle and the water flux was measured. The FRR values of the membranes are presented in Table 5. High FRR values usually indicate superior antifouling performance of a membrane. During the BSA filtration, the FRR values of the pristine PAN membrane were very low, and declined considerably from 27.3 % in the first cycle to 21.3 % and 19.0 % in the second and third filtration cycles, respec­tively, demonstrating that this membrane was susceptible to fouling. The pDA membrane showed FRR values of 83.6 %, 76.8 %, and 67.43 % after the first, second, and third filtration cycles, respectively. These FRR values are higher than those of the pristine PAN membrane, and the reduction in FRR values with increasing number of filtration cycles was probably caused by the deposition of BSA protein or the formation of a foulant cake layer over the membrane surface.

In pseudo-zwitterionic graft polymerization, the Coulombic attraction between oppositely charged, ionizable monomers (SPM and MOETMA) promotes alternating monomer incorporation during chain growth. The presence of these ionizable groups facilitates the formation of an electrostatically induced hydration layer, which enhances water absorption capacity up to seven times compared to hydrogen-bondbased hydration layers [49]. Additionally, pseudo-zwitterionic co­polymers tend to form less densely packed structures than classical zwitterionic polymers [45], allowing more water molecules to be retained. This results in a more hydrated surface layer that provides enhanced electrostatic shielding against organic foulants [50]. The pDA-SPM-MOETMA membrane demonstrated high permeation while maintaining its excellent dye rejection, over­coming the permeation and selectivity trade-off relationship. Achieving high rejection of Congo Red from dye/salt binary solutions is very challenging. Therefore, the excellent Congo Red dye rejection of 99.9 % and high water permeance of 238 ± 12 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 achieved in this study indicate good dye–salt separation compared with other reported membranes, suggesting that the combination of pDA with poly­methacrylate for membrane modification was effective. content: 4. Conclusions We developed a simple, single-step grafting procedure with inex­pensive and nontoxic materials for graft copolymerization of hydro­philic methacrylate monomers and dopamine over a porous ultrafiltration membrane. ATR–FTIR and XPS analysis showed success­ful modification of the PAN substrate by pseudo-zwitterionic pDA-SPMMOETMA with cationic (quaternary ammonium) and anionic (sulfo­nate) groups. The membranes exhibited an excellent Congo Red rejec­tion profile with high salt permeability and demonstrated outstanding antifouling performance with high FRR values of 97.3 % and 99.7 % for BSA/NaCl and Congo Red/NaCl binary feed solutions, respectively. The graft copolymerization was initiated solely by the redox-active func­tional groups of dopamine, without the need for external initiators, and was effective across a chemically diverse set of methacrylate monomers, highlighting the robustness and versatility of the method under mild aqueous conditions. The one-step grafting process, performed in aqueous solution under ambient conditions using low-cost commercial reagents, offers prom­ising potential for scale-up and compatibility with continuous mem­brane modification techniques such as roll-to-roll systems. The membranes reported here, particularly pDA-SPM-MOETMA, are prom­ising candidates for treating textile wastewater containing inorganic salts and organic dye molecules.
